phrasalVerbs.definition: To talk about someone or something in a very positive way so that people are impressed with them.
examples: The marketing team has been building up the new product launch for months to generate maximum interest.
Critics accused the media of building up the company's achievements while ignoring its shortcomings.
Before the merger, both parties worked to build up confidence among stakeholders and investors.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To give someone money so that they do not act against you.
examples: There were allegations that the corporation attempted to buy off local officials to avoid regulatory scrutiny.
The whistleblower refused to be bought off despite numerous financial incentives offered by the company.
Some believe the settlement was merely an attempt to buy off the plaintiffs before damaging evidence emerged.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To pay money to your business partner so that you can control all of a business you previously owned together.
examples: After years of disagreements, she decided to buy out her co-founder and run the company independently.
The private equity firm bought out the remaining shareholders to take the company private.
He was forced to buy out his partner when their business relationship became untenable.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To try to make people support you or buy something from you.
examples: The sales team was tasked with drumming up interest among potential investors before the IPO.
Despite extensive efforts to drum up support, the proposal failed to gain traction with the board.
She spent months drumming up business for her consulting firm in a highly competitive market.

fillInExercises.sentence: The CEO has been ___ the company's innovative approach to attract venture capital.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Build up' means to promote something positively to impress others.

fillInExercises.sentence: The corporation allegedly tried to ___ off the regulators to avoid an investigation.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Buy off' means to bribe someone so they don't act against you.

fillInExercises.sentence: When the partnership dissolved, one founder had to ___ the other to maintain control.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Buy out' means to purchase a partner's share to gain full ownership.

fillInExercises.sentence: The marketing department spent weeks trying to ___ up enthusiasm for the product launch.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Drum up' means to generate interest or support through promotional efforts.

fillInExercises.sentence: Critics suggested that the positive media coverage was designed to ___ the stock price.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Build up' refers to promoting something to create a positive impression.

fillInExercises.sentence: The acquisition allowed the parent company to ___ out all minority shareholders.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Buy out' means to purchase someone's ownership stake in a business.

fillInExercises.sentence: The sales team was struggling to ___ new clients in such a saturated market.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Drum up' means to try to generate business or support.

fillInExercises.sentence: The PR team worked tirelessly to ___ up anticipation ahead of the annual conference.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Build up' means to promote something positively over time.

lines.speaker: Emma
lines.text: Have you heard? The board is considering a proposal to [buy out] our main competitor.

lines.speaker: James
lines.text: That's ambitious. They'll need to [build up] substantial investor confidence first.

lines.speaker: Emma
lines.text: Apparently, they've already started [drumming up] support among the major shareholders.

lines.speaker: James
lines.text: I hope they're not trying to [buy off] anyone to push this through.

lines.speaker: Emma
lines.text: The legal team assures me everything is above board. They've been [building up] the case for months.

lines.speaker: James
lines.text: Still, if the deal falls through, it could damage our reputation after all this [building up].

lines.speaker: Carlos
lines.text: I've decided to [buy out] my business partner. Our visions have diverged completely.

lines.speaker: Priya
lines.text: That's a significant decision. Have you [drummed up] enough capital for the acquisition?

lines.speaker: Carlos
lines.text: The bank has been [building up] to approve the loan. I'm cautiously optimistic.

lines.speaker: Priya
lines.text: Just ensure there's no perception that you're trying to [buy off] anyone in the process.

lines.speaker: Carlos
lines.text: Absolutely. I want this transition to be entirely transparent and professional.

lines.speaker: Priya
lines.text: Once you've [bought out] the partnership, we can [drum up] new investment together.
